ref.title: Why is life built from a single molecular hand?

feed_card.headline: A molecule and its mirror image share every atom, yet one form of thalidomide was a mild sedative and the other caused about 10,000 birth defects.
teasers: Spearmint and caraway are the same molecule
Why isolating the safe form would not help
The crystals Pasteur pulled apart by hand

sections.content: A molecule and its mirror image share every atom, yet one form of thalidomide was a mild sedative and the other caused about 10,000 birth defects.

sections.content: Take a molecule and its mirror image. Every atom is the same, joined in the same order, the two as alike as your left hand and your right. Drop them in a beaker and no instrument tells them apart. They melt at the same temperature and weigh the same to the last decimal. The body is a stricter judge. It runs almost entirely on single-handed parts, and a receptor shaped to fit one mirror-form will not close on its twin. The body reads a handedness no instrument can.

content.body: In 1848 a 26-year-old chemist named Louis Pasteur sat over a microscope in Paris, sorting the crusty tartrate crystals that settle in wine casks. Two forms of the salt shared the same formula, yet one twisted polarised light while the other did nothing. Nobody could say why. Looking closer, Pasteur found the inert form was not one crystal but two, mirror images with facets angled left on some and right on the rest. He sorted them by hand with a fine pair of tweezers, a heap of left and a heap of right, and dissolved each on its own. The two piles bent light in opposite directions by the same amount: one molecule, existing as two shapes built from the same atoms mirrored. He carried it to the aging physicist Jean-Baptiste Biot, who took him by the arm: “My dear child, I have loved science so much in my life that this makes my heart pound.”

key_figures.name: Louis Pasteur
key_figures.one_line: In 1848 he sorted a tartrate salt into left and right crystals that bent light in opposite directions, the first proof that a molecule can come in mirror forms.
key_figures.image_attribution: Paul Nadar, “Louis Pasteur”, public domain (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: Thalidomide's two hands could not be kept apart
content.body: Thalidomide reached the market in 1957 as a mild sedative, sold in 46 countries and given to pregnant women for morning sickness. Its two mirror-forms are not alike: in the lab the R form calms, while the S form interferes with a growing limb. More than 10,000 children were born with shortened or missing limbs, and roughly 40% did not survive infancy. The cruelty is in the chemistry. In the body the two forms convert into each other within hours. A dose of the pure R form soon carries its share of S, so isolating the safe hand would have changed nothing. There was never a clean version to sell.

content.title: Your nose already knows the difference
content.body: You have smelled this without knowing it. One molecule, carvone, comes in two mirror-forms: one is the scent of spearmint, the other of caraway and rye bread. Same atoms, opposite hand, and the nose files them as two unrelated smells. It can do that only because the receptors inside your nose are single-handed too, each a pocket that closes on one shape and refuses its reflection. The handedness that is harmless in a spice rack is what made thalidomide two different drugs.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='600' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>A handed lock</text><path d='M56 188 H344' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.55'/><text x='200' y='212' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='middle'>receptor surface</text><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='108' cy='188' r='4'/><circle cx='200' cy='188' r='4'/></g><circle cx='292' cy='188' r='5' fill='var(--accent)'/><circle cx='200' cy='92' r='5' fill='currentColor'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'><line x1='200' y1='92' x2='110' y2='184'/><line x1='200' y1='92' x2='200' y2='184'/></g><line x1='200' y1='92' x2='290' y2='184' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='200' y='72' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85' text-anchor='middle'>one molecule</text><text x='200' y='232' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>its mirror cannot reach all three</text></svg>

content.title: Life itself is built from a single hand
content.body: Step away from medicine and the handedness runs all the way down. Every protein in every living thing is strung from left-handed amino acids, and the backbone of DNA is built from right-handed sugars, the same choice in a bacterium and in you. A mirror-image biology would work just as well on paper. Life never built it. Why the first cells committed to one hand, and how that choice got frozen in, is still unknown.

sections.content: Before thalidomide, a molecule's handedness was mostly a footnote. Chemists had known since Pasteur that molecules come in mirror forms, but a pill was made as a mixture and swallowed as one, and nobody checked the two halves apart. The catastrophe changed the rules. Since 1992 the US Food and Drug Administration has required a new drug's two mirror-forms to be tested on their own, each with its own record of benefit and risk. Most new medicines are now built as a single pure hand, and Europe's regulator has not cleared a mixed-handed one since 2016. Handedness stopped being a footnote.

content.question: Two molecules are mirror images with the exact same atoms. Why can they still act as a medicine and a poison inside the body?
options: One of them is slightly heavier than the other
The body is built from single-handed parts, so its receptors fit one mirror-form and not the other
One dissolves in blood and the other does not
The two have different melting points
content.explanation: A receptor is itself a handed shape, so it grips one mirror-form and slides off its twin. Nothing about the atoms differs. What changes is how the molecule sits in a handed pocket.

content.question: Why would selling only the sedative R form of thalidomide not have prevented the birth defects?
options: The R form is even more harmful than the S form on its own
Patients would have taken larger doses to compensate
In the body the two mirror-forms convert into each other, so a pure dose becomes a mixture
The S form is the harmless one of the two
content.explanation: Thalidomide racemises in the bloodstream within hours, turning a pure R dose into a mix of R and S. Because the body keeps regenerating the harmful hand, there was no stable safe form to isolate.

content.question: Sitting in a plain beaker with no other handed molecules around, how do two mirror-image molecules differ?
options: They have different weights and melting points
They react with ordinary acids at different speeds
One is a solid and the other a liquid
They are identical except that they rotate polarised light in opposite directions
content.explanation: In an even-handed environment the two share every measurable property, from density to boiling point. The one thing that separates them is the direction they twist polarised light, which is what Pasteur measured.

content.question: The two mirror-forms of carvone smell of spearmint and caraway. What does that reveal about the sense of smell?
options: The two forms must have different molecular weights
Smell depends mostly on temperature
The odour receptors are themselves handed, so each mirror-form fits differently
Caraway molecules are larger than spearmint ones
content.explanation: If the nose registered the two as one smell, its receptors would be even-handed. Because it tells them apart, those receptors must be single-handed shapes that respond to one mirror-form more than the other.

content.question: Pasteur separated an optically inactive tartrate into two crystal piles that bent light in opposite directions. What did this show?
options: The inactive substance was a 50/50 mix of two mirror-image forms whose rotations cancel
The two piles were entirely different chemicals
It is the crystal, not the molecule, that bends light
Tartaric acid has no handedness at all
content.explanation: Each pile alone twisted light, one left and one right, so the original was both forms together in equal amounts. Their opposite rotations had masked each other, which is why the mixture looked inactive.

content.question: Why did a drug's handedness become a routine safety question after the 1990s?
options: Single-handed molecules are cheaper to manufacture
A drug's two mirror-forms can differ in what they cure and what they harm, so each has to be tested on its own
Regulators preferred single molecules for patent reasons
Mixed-handed drugs were made illegal everywhere
content.explanation: The thalidomide disaster proved that the two hands of one molecule can carry very different effects. Since 1992 regulators have required each mirror-form to be evaluated separately for benefit and harm.

content.myth: The disaster could have been avoided by selling only thalidomide's safe mirror-form.
content.reality: The body turns one form into the other within hours, so a pure dose of the “safe” hand becomes a mixture inside the patient. There was no stable non-harmful version to isolate.

content.myth: Two mirror-image molecules are basically the same substance.
content.reality: In an even-handed setting they are identical, down to melting point and density, but living tissue is not, so the two can behave as differently as a drug and a poison.

content.myth: Thalidomide was banned and is no longer used.
content.reality: It returned. Since 1998 it has been an approved treatment for a painful complication of leprosy, and later for the blood cancer multiple myeloma, given under strict rules to prevent any pregnancy.

content.body: The chemistry of handedness is settled. Why life runs on a single hand is not. Nobody knows what tipped the earliest biology onto left-handed amino acids, or whether that tilt was chance or somehow inevitable. Closer to the clinic, predicting which mirror-form of a new molecule will help and which will harm is still a question only testing can answer.
items: Was life's single hand fixed by chance, or by some physical tilt we have not yet found?
Can the helpful and harmful mirror-forms of a drug be told apart before a person takes it?
How exactly the S form of thalidomide derails a growing limb is still being worked out.

content.label: Vantomme G. Pasteur and chirality: A story of how serendipity favors the prepared minds. Chirality 2021
Vargesson N. Thalidomide-induced teratogenesis: History and mechanisms. Birth Defects Research Part C 2015;105:140-156
Tokunaga E et al. Understanding the thalidomide chirality in biological processes by the self-disproportionation of enantiomers. Scientific Reports 2018;8:17131
US Food and Drug Administration. Development of New Stereoisomeric Drugs (policy statement, 1992)
